items.title: RT Parimal: Ask anyone about India’s space journey, & you will hear legends like Vikram Sarabhai, Homi J. Bhabha, Satish Dhawan, & A. P. J. Abdul Kal...
items.description_html:
RT Parimal
Ask anyone about India’s space journey, & you will hear legends like Vikram Sarabhai, Homi J. Bhabha, Satish Dhawan, & A. P. J. Abdul Kalam. But ask about Brahm Prakash… & the story usually begins with a Google search.

If Dhawan was the statesman, & Kalam the builder, Brahm Prakash was the alchemist. Not the man who launched rockets, the man who made them possible. He did not design trajectories, he designed the very metals that could survive them.

When Brahm Prakash (MIT educated) became the 1st Director of the Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre (VSSC) in Thumba in 1972, he inherited a collection of small, scattered units. Much of the early materials research happened in an old church building. While the West used expensive, pre-made industrial alloys, Brahm Prakash realized India could not import them due to sanctions. He set up the Propellant Fuel Complex (PFC) & the Materials & QC Group. He treated metallurgy as a Sadhana (disciplined practice), ensuring that every batch of indigenous steel was perfect.

Early SLV-3 used imported 15CDV6 steel. For larger vehicles like PSLV, stronger, lighter maraging steel (especially 18Ni M250 grade) was needed. Western export restrictions applied due to dual-use (missile/nuclear) nature. Brahm Prakash championed indigenous development at MIDHANI (Mishra Dhatu Nigam Ltd.), where he later served as Chairman (1980-84). India became 1 of the few countries with indigenous high-grade maraging steel production for rocket motor casings, a major strategic autonomy achievement still used in PSLV/GSLV.

Before space, he was a key figure under Homi Bhabha in the Department of Atomic Energy (DAE). His pioneering work on zirconium-hafnium separation (using pyro-chemical/vapour phase dechlorination methods) was original & globally acclaimed in the 1950s. Zirconium (neutron-transparent) is essential for nuclear reactor cladding/fuel; hafnium (strong neutron absorber) must be removed. He played a major role in setting up the Nuclear Fuel Complex (NFC) in Hyderabad (as Project Director) & contributed to fuel fabrication for CIRUS and later reactors.

Brahm Prakash is the reason India has Strategic Autonomy. Because he mastered the materials, India did not have to beg the world for the special metals required to build the GSLV/the Agni missiles.

One in two grid operators have insufficient grid capacity to connect planned wind and solar. But grids must be renewables-ready to protect against volatile power prices.

items.title: NEW | Drax received nearly £1 BILLION in public subsidies for biomass burning in 2025, despite being the UK’s largest emitter for 10 years running. ...
items.description_html:
NEW | Drax received nearly £1 BILLION in public subsidies for biomass burning in 2025, despite being the UK’s largest emitter for 10 years running.

Payments will halve from 2027, but Drax is set to remain the UK’s largest emitter until at least 2030.

items.title: 📢 We’re looking for a writer to join our team at Our World in Data! → https://ourworldindata.org/hiring-writer-2026 What we’re looking for is qu...
items.description_html:
📢 We’re looking for a writer to join our team at Our World in Data!

→ https://ourworldindata.org/hiring-writer-2026

What we’re looking for is quite unique: someone who writes excellent narrative articles on large global problems, finds memorable framings to make hard ideas easier to understand, while being genuinely obsessed with technical details.

They need to know which questions are important and what readers need to understand the answers.

If this sounds like you (or someone you know!), you can find more details from our Deputy Editor, @_HannahRitchie, at the link here.

items.title: RT Nitin Pai: The headline misses a very important point about infrastructure: You must build it ahead of demand! The problem in India is the opposite...
items.description_html:
RT Nitin Pai
The headline misses a very important point about infrastructure:

You must build it ahead of demand!

The problem in India is the opposite of what the headline suggests: when we build, we build late and quickly run out of capacity.


BBC News (World): India has splurged billions on metro trains. But where are the commuters? https://bbc.in/4mIaUcL

items.title: RT Megha Patnaik: Happy to see my work in @thewire_in GST’s key feature: it’s self-enforcing. Firms need invoices to claim credits → they demand co...
items.description_html:
RT Megha Patnaik
Happy to see my work in @thewire_in

GST’s key feature: it’s self-enforcing. Firms need invoices to claim credits → they demand compliance from suppliers → formalisation cascades through supply chains.

https://thewire.in/economy/how-the-gst-created-a-formalisation-cascade

from @Ideas4India

Paper
https://meghapatnaik.github.io/research/gst_prowess.pdf

items.description_html:
RT Megha Patnaik
Thrilled to share our paper on Sustainable Remote Workplaces (with @BeataJechorek , @asdurso & @ChloeThurstonDC ) is forthcoming in JSIS.

We show employees evaluate remote work as bundles of trade-offs: flexibility, pay, monitoring, & costs are considered jointly

#FutureOfWork

items.title: RT Megha Patnaik: new blog post at @Ideas4India 45% of India’s manufacturing is informal. My work shows GST is slowly changing this: an input credit ...
items.description_html:
RT Megha Patnaik
new blog post at @Ideas4India 45% of India’s manufacturing is informal. My work shows GST is slowly changing this: an input credit system creates a formalisation cascade where firms demand invoices from suppliers & this pulls the entire supply chain slowly into the formal economy


Ideas for India: Value-added tax has a self-enforcing design that generates formalisation cascades through supply chains. Using @_CMIE Prowess data on 12,024 firms for the period 2013-2022, Megha Patnaik @megonomista examines impact of India’s introduction of GST in 2017
https://www.ideasforindia.in/topics/macroeconomics/how-the-gst-created-a-formalisation-cascade

items.title: RT Megha Patnaik: Global Capability Centres are projected to contribute to 3.5% of India's GDP. My new paper argues that GCCs are firm-level reorganis...
items.description_html:
RT Megha Patnaik
Global Capability Centres are projected to contribute to 3.5% of India's GDP. My new paper argues that GCCs are firm-level reorganisation at the global scale: only the most productive firms set up GCCs, trading off lower wages against higher coordination costs of remote work.


CEPR: New CEPR Discussion Paper - DP21294
Services Across Borders: How Firms Organize Remote Work at Scale
Megha Patnaik @megonomista @UniLUISS
https://ow.ly/2gIf50YuL4m
#CEPR_ITRE #EconTwitter

items.title: RT Megha Patnaik: 45% of India's GDP is informal. My new paper shows GST's credit mechanism is pulling firms into the formal economy, not through enfo...
items.description_html:
RT Megha Patnaik
45% of India's GDP is informal. My new paper shows GST's credit mechanism is pulling firms into the formal economy, not through enforcement but because every buyer demands an invoice from the supplier behind it.
Formalisation cascading begins with large firms and grows upstream


CEPR: New CEPR Discussion Paper - DP21163
Self-Enforcing Tax Design and Supply Chain Formalization: Evidence from India’s GST Reform
Megha Patnaik @megonomista @UniLUISS
https://ow.ly/5QK550YfcL1
#CEPR_PE #EconTwitter

items.description_html:
RT Megha Patnaik
new blog post at @Ideas4India 45% of India’s manufacturing is informal. My work shows GST is slowly changing this: an input credit system creates a formalisation cascade where firms demand invoices from suppliers & this pulls the entire supply chain slowly into the formal economy


Ideas for India: Value-added tax has a self-enforcing design that generates formalisation cascades through supply chains. Using @_CMIE Prowess data on 12,024 firms for the period 2013-2022, Megha Patnaik @megonomista examines impact of India’s introduction of GST in 2017
https://www.ideasforindia.in/topics/macroeconomics/how-the-gst-created-a-formalisation-cascade

items.title: RT cecfee: Heat’s impact on students is well documented. But what about early childhood, when cognitive foundations are formed? A new study by Bensto...
items.description_html:
RT cecfee
Heat’s impact on students is well documented. But what about early childhood, when cognitive foundations are formed?

A new study by Benston John, E. Somanathan and Rohini Somanathan shows that a simple intervention -painting anganwadi roofs white - reduced indoor heat and improved children’s cognitive performance by 6–7%.

In a new article of @Ideas4India, the authors argue that findings from this study are striking, not just because of the learning gains, but because it comes from a low-cost intervention (around ₹1 per child per day) that requires no ongoing energy use.

Read the article: https://www.ideasforindia.in/topics/human-development/cooling-classrooms-to-protect-human-capital-evidence-from-keralas-anganwadis

Link to the study: https://www.isid.ac.in/~cecfee/publication/when-the-roof-reflects-heat-learning-and-adaptation-in-early-childhood-settings/

@emc_kerala | Environment for Development

items.title: RT Ali Hashem علي هاشم: Most important takeaway from Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf’s interview with State TV, “We made progress in the Islamabad n...
items.description_html:
RT Ali Hashem علي هاشم
Most important takeaway from Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf’s interview with State TV, “We made progress in the Islamabad negotiations, but the gaps are wide and some fundamental points remain.”

MBG covered several topics in what could be described as a message before a turning point, tone was explanatory, less rhetorical, carried both political and strategic messages.

Here are points I found interesting:

📌 It is clear that the enemy was defeated, but this is different from saying that we destroyed their army. When the enemy does not achieve its goals, it means it has been defeated

📌The enemy intended to bring counter-revolutionaries into the country from our western and eastern borders to create chaos inside, but it did not succeed and was defeated.

📌All the enemy's effort was to impose their demands on us, and it is important that we assert our rights; therefore, negotiation is a method of struggle here.

📌Securing the nation's rights must be our main goal, and rest assured that there will be no surrender in the diplomatic arena.

📌I emphasize that we have always sought to normalize transit through the Strait of Hormuz and still do; if it has stopped now, it is because the ceasefire was not fully established in Lebanon.

📌If we were negotiating with the US at the time Trump said so, then why were there so many ultimatums? Why were they threatening to strike our infrastructure?

📌 We did not conduct any negotiations until 48 hours before the ceasefire. Trump is a master of lying and psychological warfare. Yesterday he posted 8 tweets, 7 of which were lies.

📌 Our negotiations began on Saturday when we arrived in Pakistan, and before that, only in the last 48 hours of the war leading to the ceasefire, messages were exchanged through Pakistan.

📌Our approach was that our entry into the 10-point negotiation framework would be when the Lebanon ceasefire was established and Iran's frozen assets were released.

📌Basically, our first meeting with Mr. Asim Munir as a mediator lasted 2 hours and 15 minutes in Islamabad, focusing on the Lebanon ceasefire. His first meeting with the Americans was also on this subject. From there, we saw their efforts to pursue the ceasefire begin, and they implemented the ceasefire incompletely.

📌Currently, we also have disagreements on several issues related to the nuclear matter and the Strait of Hormuz. Of course, the essence of negotiation is for understanding, but we have our principles.

📌The Zionist regime and America have constantly had a cycle of war - ceasefire - peace, and this cycle must be broken, and sustainable peace must be established with guarantees that war will not happen again.

📌We have reached conclusions on some issues, but not on others, and other proposals have been made; we are still far from a final discussion.

📌We insist on some issues which are non-negotiable for us, and their number is one or two, and the other side also has similar points.

📌In summary, we made progress in the Islamabad negotiations, but the gaps are wide and some fundamental points remain.

📌We have tried to follow the experiences of past periods and the distrust towards the U.S., and (in case of agreement) our policy will also be step-by-step action; it should not be that we fulfill our commitments but they do not; in case of agreement, we take one step and they must also take one step.

items.title: RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش: I don’t know what has been presented in the White House, but this is the adversary st...
items.description_html:
RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش
I don’t know what has been presented in the White House, but this is the adversary status I would present, with one important
Takeaway - At this stage, the most important requirement for U.S. policy is strategic realism about what the use of force can still achieve against Iran.

1. Iran’s leadership is not interested in prolonging the war, but it believes it holds an advantage in a “pain tolerance” competition vis-à-vis the United States and the international community.

2. Threats of force are unlikely to produce meaningful concessions in negotiations. Iran is expected to hold firmly to the red lines it has maintained from the outset.

3. Maritime pressure will not compel Iranian capitulation. Absent an agreement, it is more likely to lead to direct confrontation between the U.S. and Iran. Interdictions of Iranian shipping may accelerate escalation, but not surrender.

4. The current Iranian leadership will not give up uranium enrichment, its missile program, support for its regional network of proxies, or its strategic influence over the Strait of Hormuz.

5. Limited strikes will not alter this trajectory. Tehran is not easily swayed by signaling. A broader campaign against Iran’s civilian infrastructure could severely damage its economy, but would almost certainly trigger wide-scale retaliation, particularly against energy infrastructure across the Gulf.

6. Without regime change, which would require a dramatic U.S. investment in time, resources, and political will, these core dynamics are unlikely to shift.

7. Decision-making in Iran is becoming more fragmented and hardline, increasingly shaped by the IRGC. This makes coherent compromise more difficult than in the past.

8. What Iran did not concede before or during the conflict, it is even less likely to concede now, regardless of military pressure or blockade measures.

9. In the absence of a deal, escalation is not a risk, it is the default trajectory. Iran is already preparing for it, including efforts to rebuild and reinforce its missile capabilities.

10. To date, even successful operational achievements have not meaningfully changed Iran’s strategic calculus. Paradoxically, the campaign has weakened Iran economically, but strengthened the regime internally, especially among the regime supporters.

+1

The administration seems to have two main options: a framework agreement broadly resembling the previous nuclear deal, Or a wider escalation would impose massive economic costs on Iran, but rather than restrain it, it could drive a significant expansion of its escalatory actions.

#IranWar‌

Beginning today, businesses can file claims through a new customs system.

Over 330,000 importers across 53 million shipments are expected to be eligible.

Once approved, refunds plus interest will be paid within 60 to 90 days.

items.title: The US wage growth gap is historically wide: After-tax wages for higher-income households rose +5.6% YoY in March on a 3-month moving average basis, t...
items.description_html:
The US wage growth gap is historically wide:

After-tax wages for higher-income households rose +5.6% YoY in March on a 3-month moving average basis, the highest in at least 3 years.

At the same time, lower-income earners saw their wages grow just+1.0% YoY, near the lowest in at least 3 years.

This marks the widest gap between the highest and lowest earners since data began in 2015.

By comparison, middle-income wages grew +2.0% YoY last month, near the lowest since mid-2024.

Meanwhile, US CPI inflation jumped +3.3% YoY in March, meaning lower and middle-income households are seeing their real wages decline.

In other words, inflation is eating all of the wage gains for the bottom two-thirds of American households.

Asset owners are the only winners.

items.title: The global smartphone market is facing a historic decline: Global smartphone shipments are set to fall -13% YoY in 2026, or -160 million units, to ~1....
items.description_html:
The global smartphone market is facing a historic decline:

Global smartphone shipments are set to fall -13% YoY in 2026, or -160 million units, to ~1.1 billion, according to IDC.

This marks a sharp drop from a +2% growth in 2025 and +6% in 2024.

The decline is being driven by an unprecedented memory chip shortage that is inflating component costs across the industry.

As a result, smartphone makers are discontinuing unprofitable entry-level models and pushing consumers toward higher-priced devices.

Last year, ~170 million smartphones shipped for under $100, a segment currently uneconomical to maintain.

The shortage is expected to persist into mid-2027, and even when supply returns, memory prices are unlikely to fall back to 2025 levels.

The days of cheap smartphones are over.

items.title: Market liquidity is improving: Top of book depth in the S&P 500 futures jumped to $10.8 million in the week ending Tuesday, the highest since January....
items.description_html:
Market liquidity is improving:

Top of book depth in the S&P 500 futures jumped to $10.8 million in the week ending Tuesday, the highest since January.

This marks a +440% increase from the ~$2.0 million low recorded last month.

The current reading is also higher than 84.6% of all observations over the last 2 years.

By comparison, the peak over this period was ~$17.0 million, recorded during the market's rally following President Trump's 2024 election victory.

ETFs accounted for ~28% of total volume in the week ending Tuesday, in-line with the historical average, after exceeding 40% for most of March.

Falling ETF trading volumes are typically associated with calmer markets and greater investor confidence in individual stock selection.

Market conditions are stabilizing.

items.title: Retail investors are leading the historic rally: Retail's favorite stocks are outperforming mutual fund favorite names by +11 percentage points so far...
items.description_html:
Retail investors are leading the historic rally:

Retail's favorite stocks are outperforming mutual fund favorite names by +11 percentage points so far in April.

This puts retail on track for their best relative month since November 2020 after 5 consecutive months of relative underperformance.

As a result, the basket of stocks most favored by retail investors has rallied +22% since late March.

Furthermore, the Meme Stock ETF, $MEME, has surged +56% since March 31st, on track for its best monthly performance since its inception in October 2025.

Bullish retail investor sentiment is surging.

BREAKING: Iranian state media calls reports of another round of US-Iran talks in Pakistan “false” and issues a statement.

The statement says:

“US excessive demands, unreasonable and unrealistic expectations, frequent shifts in positions, continuous contradictions, and the so-called naval blockade which violates ceasefire understanding along with threatening rhetoric, have so far hindered progress in the negotiations.

Under these conditions, the outlook for constructive talks remains bleak.

The news published by the US is part of their propaganda campaign and a ‘blame game’ aimed at pressuring Iran.”

2. The US claimed peace talks would resume with Iran tomorrow

3. Iran has backed out of peace talks with the US

4. Iran has accused the US of plotting a “surprise attack”

5. The US has struck and seized an Iranian vessel in the Strait of Hormuz

6. Iran has denied Trump’s claims of giving up uranium enrichment

There appears to be severe distrust on both sides of the table in this conflict.

Trump says “no deal” this week would be detrimental for Iran.

Futures open in 2 hours.

items.title: Finally some good progress on Market Coupling - Round Robin system is no more - Grid India takes Operator status to match bids as a neutral party - Gr...
items.description_html:
Finally some good progress on Market Coupling

- Round Robin system is no more
- Grid India takes Operator status to match bids as a neutral party
- Grid India has 6 months to come up with the Market coupling algorithm similar to Europe's Day Ahead Market
- Post 6 months, then after everyone's approval they have to build the systems, the infrastructure, cybersecurity protection etc etc

Looks like this should take 12 to 18months to see some value

This is assuming IEX does not put new cases on the order to stall

Now contrary to popular belief - Market Coupling like the EU is actually good

- EU trading Market boomed post market Coupling
- The industry leader EPEX Spot who was a market leader before coupling still has most of the volume
- EU has only 2 players taking all the market share with Nord Pool being 2nd

IEX
- Yes market coupling without round Robin is good, but market coupling itself will take some market share
- At best market share should dip from 99% to 75%.
- At best post market coupling they would stall revenues for 1 year
- Post market coupling the regulator will boost Power trading a lot
- Volume will rise
- News instruments like CfD coming to power exchanges

Verdict
Short term IEX will be volatile
For long term investors the game will start post Market Coupling

Today Indias largest Power exchange is at close to a billion dollars.

Disc - Invested in IEX at Lower Levels. Not a buy or sell reco

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: Nomura on Yes Bk Neutral, TP Rs 21 4Q26: Improving fundamentals; core-ROAs still catching up Broad-based strength across growth, margi...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
Nomura on Yes Bk
Neutral, TP Rs 21
4Q26: Improving fundamentals; core-ROAs still catching up
Broad-based strength across growth, margins and profitability
ROA at 0.95%; SR recoveries to moderate from FY26 levels
Levers for profitability at play, but core-ROA profile build up would take time
Strategic execution of new management is a key monitorable

MS on Yes Bk
UW. TP Rs 15
Core PPOP was in line vs MSe.
PAT miss of 2% was due to Rs3.4bn one-off provisions.
Credit costs were stable, aided by recoveries.
NIM improved QoQ and was above MSe.
Balance sheet growth improved to 11% YoY (3% in F3Q)
Stay UW given valuation significantly above fundamentals.

JPM on Yes BK
UW, TP Rs 18
operating performance in
4QFY26 beat JPMe with PAT of Rs 10.7bn (+45% y/y, +12% q/q) coming 9% above JPMe, driven by
(i) cost control – opex rose by just 2% YoY, and came 2% lower vs JPMe.
(ii) CIR improved to 63%, but remains elevated vs peers and continues to weigh on RoA.
(iii) 28bps of credit costs booked during qtr were 4bps lower vs. JPMe
Yes Bank achieved an RoA of 1% for first time since reconstruction, however, supported by 31bps of contribution from SR recoveries
Believe that improving visibility on sustainability of these levels remains key, as risks from MSMEs are likely to emerge from supply chain disruptions, driving credit costs higher
Believe valuations at 1.2x P/B FY27E already price in much of b/s growth & RoA improvements.

@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh @latha_venkatesh

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: UBS on HDFC BK Buy, TP Rs 1175 Q4 PAT beat driven by lower provisions Loans grew c12% YoY; LDR down c400bp QoQ to 94.6% Management exp...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
UBS on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 1175
Q4 PAT beat driven by lower provisions
Loans grew c12% YoY; LDR down c400bp QoQ to 94.6%
Management expects loan growth momentum to sustain
Expect c14-15% ROE in FY27-28E

MS on HDFC BK
OW. TP Rs 1025
PAT beat MSe by 4% as sharply lower provisions and lower operating costs offset slightly lower core revenue (2% below MSe).
Commentary was anchored on loan growth with LDR no longer a constraint.
Earnings resilience and attractive valuation make it one of key picks amid geopolitical risks.

CLSA on HDFC BK
O-P, TP Rs 1200
4QFY26 NII and PPOP were largely in line, while PBT was a 4% beat
Would characterise it as a steady performance in a slightly challenging quarter.
Loan growth of 12% YoY was largely in line
Sequential quarterly-average (QA) deposit growth picked up from the past couple of quarters.
LDR (a metric we consider irrelevant, but many investors still find important) declined to 95% from 99% in prior quarter.
Margins were up 3bp QoQ, a decent outcome in this environment when cost of wholesale deposits rose sharply post the Gulf conflict.
HDFCB’s gross slippages have declined YoY on an absolute basis for three consecutive quarters.

JPM on HDFC BK
OW, TP Rs 990
Delivered a steady 4Q26 with PAT broadly in line vs JPMe.
While NII (-4% vs JPMe) and NIMs trailed JPMe on elevated competitive intensity and an adverse product mix, they were offset by lower than expected loan loss provisions.
NII growth for HDFCB has remained volatile, decelerating to 3.2% y/y in 4Q26 vs 4.8%/6.4% in 2Q/3Q, respectively
Expect NII growth to accelerate in FY27/28 on a better-than-expected pick-up in system credit growth and funding cost benefits from a decline in the share of higher cost borrowings.
Decline in average LCR to 114% (vs 116% in 3Q, 110-120% target range) does reduce balance-sheet flexibility to support margins/RoA in FY27

Bernstein on HDFC BK
O-P, TP Rs 1150
There was no dramatic improvement, but neither was there much to complain about, especially given b/s adjustment in progress.
While growth trends were largely known - slightly weaker loan growth & still healthy deposit growth driving an improvement in LDR - margins showed sequential resilience despite the decline in LDR
Earnings profile was supported by a sharp decline in credit costs & continued cost discipline, which helped offset moderation in non-interest income
As a result, bank delivered steady profitability, with RoA holding at 1.9% & EPS growth at 9% YoY (vs. consensus estimates of 6%).

Jefferies on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 1050
Q4 profit of Rs192bn, up 9% YoY, was in line with estimates.
Topline (NII & Fees) was slightly softer than estimates, but was compensated by operating synergies, lower credit costs & higher treasury gains.
Improved deposit growth of 14% aided loan growth of 12% and ~400bps QoQ fall in LDR.
Momentum in deposit growth & NIMs should be key drivers
Tweak estimates & see 15% CAGR in PBT (ex-treasury)

Nomura on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 950
Soft delivery on NIMs; lower credit costs drive PAT beat
Deposit growth keyamid tight liquidity buffers
Asset quality pristine; healthy loan and deposit growth
Leadership continuity and succession planning remain a key monitorable, with tenure of MD & CEO coming up for renewal (in Oct-26), alongside appointment of a new part-time chairman. Expect HDFCB to deliver RoA/RoE of 1.8%/14% over FY27-28F

Investec on HDFC BK
Buy, TP Rs 915
A soft operational quarter in 4Q, with a subdued core PPOP performance (+3% YoY, 3% below est) driven by soft NII (+3% YoY) and fees (+6% YoY).
PAT rose 9% YoY to Rs 192bn, with RoA steady at 1.8% and broadly in line with estimates, aided by lower credit costs of 36 bps.
Loan growth remained steady at 12% YoY (+4% QoQ), while deposits grew strongly at 14% YoY (8.6% QoQ), lowering the CD ratio to 95%, with CASA deposits up 10% QoQ to 34.1%.
Deposit growth was aided by a strong periodend balance build up, as avg deposits/CASA growth was lower at 3.6% / 2.2% QoQ
LCR moderated further to 113.5% in 4Q (vs 116% as of 3Q).

@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh @latha_venkatesh

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: CITI on ICICI BK Buy, TP Rs 1720 4Q RoA of 2.4% surpassed CitiE, underpinned by near-zero credit cost (3bps), stable NIMs, and acceler...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
CITI on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1720
4Q RoA of 2.4% surpassed CitiE, underpinned by near-zero credit cost (3bps), stable NIMs, and accelerated loan growth of 15.8% YoY/6.0% QoQ.
Positives
i) Broad-based growth momentum — led by rural (gold loans) and business banking, alongside retail (mortgages/PL/CV/CE) too stepping up; corporate portfolio expanded 3% QoQ.
ii) Elevated corporate recoveries, declining retail unsecured stress contained credit cost.
iii) 5bps IT refund interest lifted NIMs by 2bps.
Offsets:
i) Modest fee growth (7% YoY).
ii) Rs1.06bn treasury loss.
iii) Overhead costs rise (+14% YoY). iv) Deposits growth of 10.8% YoY/3.8% QoQ.
Outlook: Raise loan growth estimates to 15-16%, expect range-bound NIMs, agri-PSL provision reversal offers credit cost buffer.
Earnings estimates broadly retained — credit cost comfort, opex leverage, and improved growth offset by stable NIM and modest non-interest income.

CLSA on ICICI BK
O-P, TP Rs 1700
4QNII & PPOP were largely in line with estimates, while PBT was a 10% above due to negligible credit costs
Latter was driven by sharp recoveries from written-off accounts.
However, even excluding that, core asset quality was better than estimates
Key +ve was pickup in loan growth, up from 12% YoY last quarter to 16% YoY in 4QFY26
This should put to rest worries that ICICIB’s loan growth is slowing.
NIM was largely stable QoQ; a decent outcome in this environment.
Fee income remains sluggish as credit card spending is muted.
Lastly, asset quality continues to surprise positively with the gross/net slippage ratios improving 40bps/10bps YoY.

Nomura on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1620
4QFY26: Loan growth re-accelerates, credit costs surprise
Normalised PAT beat of 2%
Asset quality pristine; loan growth re-accelerates
With LCR remaining comfortable at 124%, BK is well positioned to defend near-term loan growth and NIMs
FY27-28F EPS are broadly unchanged, as lower credit costs assumptions are offset by softer fee income
Expect BK to deliver sector-leading 2.2% RoA & 16% RoE over FY27-28F.

Jefferies on ICICI Bk
Buy, TP Rs 1670
Q4 profit of Rs137bn, up 8% YoY, was ahead of estimates aided by significantly lower credit costs helped by some corporate recoveries and lower slippages in unsecured loans.
Pickup in loan growth to 16% is encouraging, aided by deposit growth of 11% and headroom in LDR now at 87% (83% last year)
See 13% CAGR in PBT (ex-treasury) over FY26-29E & ROE of 16% in FY27E.
Valuation at 2.2x FY27E adj PB is at deserved premium to peers.

Kotak Inst Eqt on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1800
Delivered 8% yoy earnings growth in 4QFY26 led by a sharp yoy decline in provisions.
Headline loan growth (16% yoy) and deposit growth (11% yoy) have improved, but operating profit growth was weak (3% yoy).
Asset quality indicators stayed stable.
As a sector leader, ICICI Bank has to deal with elevated expectations that are challenging to meet.
Combined with its valuation premium, this makes outperformance difficult
Like balance sheet resilience over growth.

Investec on ICICI BK
Buy, TP raised to Rs 1625
A strong Q4FY26, with PAT up 8% YoY and 21% QoQ to Rs 137bn (+6% ahead of est) with best-in-class RoA delivery of 2.4%.
Importantly, ICICI delivered strong NII growth (+8% YoY and 4.8% QoQ) with a sharp pickup in loan growth (+16% YoY vs 11.5% YoY as of 3Q) and stable NIMs (at 4.32%).
Loan growth was strong and broadbased at 16% YoY, led by business banking (+24% YoY) and rural loans (+26% YoY).
Core fee growth was however soft at 6% YoY, and higher opex capped core PPoP growth at 5% YoY.
Asset quality remained robust, with net slippages at just 35bp (annualized), and credit cost at just 3bp (vs 37bp in 3Q), helping drive 30bp QoQ RoA expansion

MS on ICICI Bk
OW TP Rs 1705
PAT beat MSe by 7%, largely driven by lower provisions.
Core PPOP was 1% above MSe, reflecting higher NII.
Adjusted NIM dipped 2bp QoQ to 4.27% (MSe 4.25%).
Loan growth accelerated to 16% vs. 10% in 2QF26.
Resilience with growth amid geopolitical uncertainties -

Bernstein on ICICI BK
O-P, TP Rs 1550
Q4 addressed key concern—loan growth—with bank delivering 16% YoY growth after several quarters of trailing system
However, deposit growth remained modest, particularly relative to system, possibly reflecting a conscious effort to protect margins
Overall, PPoP growth was somewhat subdued, weighed down by weak noninterest income & steady cost growth
A sharp decline in credit costs, however, provided a meaningful offset, supporting earnings growth.
As a result, profitability improved, with RoA expanding to 2.4% despite modest underlying operating trends.

JPM on ICICI BK
OW. TP Rs 1600
Delivered another quarter of industry leading growth with net advances up by 6% q/q, which came in better vs JPMe at 4.6% and vs large private banks at ~4% q/q
Thesis of positive inflection in NII growth was visible in 4Q as well with NII rising by 8.4% y/y vs 7.7%/7.4% over the last 2 quarters.
With probability of further rate cuts reducing significantly, expect NII growth to accelerate further to 12.3%/15.1% in FY28/28
Also, ICICIB affirmed no concerns in its loan portfolio (especially Business Banking which is 21% of advances) from the ongoing supply chain disruptions from the West Asia conflict, which is comforting

UBS on ICICI BK
Buy, TP Rs 1720
Q4 PAT beat driven by negligible provisions; Margins expand 2bp QoQ
Loan growth at c6% QoQ; LDR down 170bp
Bank confident on loan growth while NIMs to be range-bound
Est RoA of c2.2% (FY27-29E) for ICICI & expect most metrics to remain steady
STK trading at c2.x March 2027 PBV
@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh @latha_venkatesh

items.title: Samsung Electronics is currently trading at nearly 5x fwd earnings That looks surprising despite the stock being up ~300% YoY Reason - earnings have b...
items.description_html:
Samsung Electronics is currently trading at nearly 5x fwd earnings

That looks surprising despite the stock being up ~300% YoY

Reason - earnings have been extremely strong, driven by the memory upcycle due to AI led demand for HBM & DRAM

Memory is a highly cyclical industry that goes through deep cycles

When the cycle is in an upturn, P/E looks very low because earnings are strong

When the cycle turns down, P/E starts looking high

So a low P/E here does not automatically mean cheap - it usually reflects where we are in the upcycle

Let’s see how this plays out from here

items.title: Imagine a Sector where Companies: 1⃣Operate in a regulated oligopoly with very high entry barriers 2⃣Cash Rich B/S with Healthy Dividend payout rati...
items.description_html:
Imagine a Sector where Companies:

1⃣Operate in a regulated oligopoly with very high entry barriers
2⃣Cash Rich B/S with Healthy Dividend payout ratio of 50%+
3⃣20%+ Return on capital employed
4⃣Convert 100% of Operating profit into OCF (FCF machines)

Sounds like a dream right?

That is exactly the structure of Credit Rating Agencies

Three key listed players in this space:
1⃣CRISIL - subsidiary of S&P Global
2⃣ICRA - subsidiary of Moody's
3⃣CARE Ratings - Indian promoter led independent rating agency
4⃣India Ratings - subsidiary of Fitch Ratings (unlisted)

Rating agencies are capital light financial infrastructure businesses. Their core role is to assess credit risk of companies, banks, NBFCs, bonds, structured products & government backed instruments.

They sit at the center of the debt market ecosystem.

Whenever companies raise debt, refinance loans, issue bonds, securitize pools or structure instruments – Ratings are required.

This is a regulated oligopoly with global parentage, strong credibility backing, and very high reputation led entry barriers. Once credibility is established, the business becomes sticky with strong operating leverage.

Their Primary REV streams include
- Credit rating of corporate debt instruments, bank loan ratings, structured finance ratings, SME ratings, monitoring fees on outstanding rated instruments, research and analytics services, risk advisory and consulting

Most importantly
Once a rating is assigned, companies continue paying annual surveillance fees
This creates recurring REV visibility.

This makes them indirect beneficiaries of:
- Credit growth, bond market expansion, securitisation activity, structured finance growth & regulatory compliance requirements

This creates a steady stream of issuer paid rating income along with recurring surveillance revenue.
As credit markets deepen, rating activity increases.

What is becoming interesting now across all Rating agencies is the visible acceleration in growth.

With system credit growth moving back to mid teens, bond markets expanding, securitisation activity improving & infrastructure financing picking up - Operating environment for rating agencies is turning favourable again

This is a space worth studying.

items.title: RT Nithin Kamath: I recently had dinner with Dr Devi Shetty, the founder of Narayana Hospitals. For those who don't know him, he's the guy who figured...
items.description_html:
RT Nithin Kamath
I recently had dinner with Dr Devi Shetty, the founder of Narayana Hospitals. For those who don't know him, he's the guy who figured out how to do open heart surgery for a few hundred dollars when the same procedure costs a bomb in the US. Narayana has 18,000 beds across India, and if you ask most middle-class people in Bangalore about it, they'll speak highly of it.

There was one thing I kept thinking about over and over again after meeting him.

Narayana's market cap is around ₹38,000 crore. Now compare that to pretty much any half-decent financial services business in India, and it'll be valued more than that, including Zerodha. A brokerage, worth more than a hospital chain, that has probably saved hundreds of thousands of lives.

I get the arguments. If you're a fund manager/analyst, you can immediately explain it away using margins, capex, asset-light vs asset-heavy, and all that, and I'm not saying the market is wrong.

But it's still a strange world we've built, where the businesses closest to money get valued the highest, and the ones doing the hard and essential things get priced like boring utilities. A hospital carries physical infrastructure, enormous liability, thin margins and the actual weight of keeping people alive. And somehow that's worth less than a platform for buying and selling stocks.

I don't have a clean take on this. All of this just felt odd.

Ps: Nothing here is investment advice. For that, go to @zerodhavarsity

items.title: RT Manan S. Bhatt: "Ambushed" #UntoldStoryArmy During a military training course in Mhow, India, the 1962 war with China broke out. My course was canc...
items.description_html:
RT Manan S. Bhatt
"Ambushed"
#UntoldStoryArmy

During a military training course in Mhow, India, the 1962 war with China broke out. My course was cancelled, and everyone returned to their units. However, I received special orders to stay put and await further instructions. Two days later, my posting to Jammu and Kashmir arrived – with immediate reporting required.

Having driven my car to Mhow, I had no choice but to take it back to Punjab, leave it there, and then travel on to J&K. Pressed for time, I decided to drive straight from Mhow to Delhi, a risky decision. One particularly dangerous stretch was between Gwalior and Agra, notorious for bandits and lawlessness.

Traveling alone at night through this area was foolish, but I pressed on in my trusty Hindustan-14. Around midnight, approaching a narrow pass through low hills, I found the road blocked by a fallen tree. It was an ambush. Turning back wasn't an option.

As I stopped, armed men materialized, their faces hidden. They searched me for valuables, taking my watch and wallet before moving on to my car and luggage. However, things took an unexpected turn when someone announced a "Sardar" had arrived.

Every one pulled back a little and there appeared a sturdy young man with a large mustache. As he drew near, he suddenly sprung to attention and gave me a smart salute and said ‘Sahib Ji, tussee ethay ki kar rahey ho.’ (Sir what are you doing here!)

From his voice and his face, latter day mustache notwithstanding, I recognized him. He was my tank gunner and had been discharged after his seven-year tenure of engagement. Those days soldiers were discharged after seven years and given no pension.

I responded to his query and asked him,’ Nahar Singh toon aye ki kam pharya hai (Nahar Singh what is this work you have taken on.) He said, ‘Sahib Ji admi nu kuch na kuch taan karna chaheda hai, welay beth kaay vi ki karna hai.’ (Sir one must do, some or the other work and there is no point in sitting idle!)

I told him, ‘tenu aasi eh taan nahi sakhlaya’ (we did not train you for such activity.) “Sahib ji tohati ambush laney di sikhlai kam ahh rahi hai. Sadi ambush wich koe nuks hai!.” (Sir your training in laying an ambush is proving useful. Do you find any fault with our ambush.)

He responded with a loud chuckle. He addressed his gang and said. “Dekheya sadhi regiment de officer kiney deelair hun, ekelay he rat nu is sarak tay chal rahey hun.” (do you see how brave are the officers of our regiment. They travel alone on this road at night.)

I asked him if his wife knew what work he was doing. To this he answered “Sarian gallaan wife nu thorian dassidian hun.

Oh soch di hai kay main police da kam kar rahaan hain.” (You don’t disclose everything to your wife. She thinks I am working for the police). At this one of them interjected that, “Police whi ehhoo kam kardi hai.” (Police too does this sort of work).

This raised much laughter amongst them. Then he told his men not to just stand watching but to quickly make tea for the sahib. All my stuff was put back in the car, and my watch and wallet returned. After a cup of tea and much bonhomie and handshaking I took leave of Nahar Singh and his gang.

A story by Lt Gen Harwant Singh

items.title: RT Andrew Peretti: Sure - a Tax & STT reduction will help, but where are the blue sky capital market reforms? We need policy mavericks out there willi...
items.description_html:
RT Andrew Peretti
Sure - a Tax & STT reduction will help, but where are the blue sky capital market reforms? We need policy mavericks out there willing to advocate for bold reform for the benefit of the whole market. Can't you be one of them @Nithin0dha

A few of my personal ideas for whatever little it is worth:

1) Liberalised and streamlined regime for foreign retail investors? I'd love to invest, but no thanks to going through the lengthy FPI registration process to make retail size investments. Nothing would boost volumes or goodwill more than this.

2) Allow FPI/MF to intraday trade to promote cash market liquidity. Make cash great again. Why can you trade F&O intraday but not cash as an FPI, for example?

3) Create stock lending pools at FPI/ODI issuers, by allowing OTC stock borrowing/synthetic access. (Complex topic, DM me)

4) Allow gross custodian funding for FPIs and even broker funding for well capitalized intermediaries (...no, not just for failed DVP trades) in order to create capital efficiency for investors.

5) Create an after hours cash market to help overseas/domestics to manage overnight risk and spur round the clock trading? (To the 8-4pm brokers: a couple of hours a week on rotation will be good for you :))

6) Futures - given the focus on the linear market, why not allow futures on every BSE 500 stock or more? (Look at the US, nearly every company has listed options)

7) Local Funds- I have friends running PMS/AIFs. The cost is attractive versus other jurisdictions, but they feel suffocated by the bureaucracy. So much to learn from some of the overseas fund jurisdictions, where private funds can be setup with minimal compliance burden and tax efficiency.

8) Allow unsponsored ADRs/GDRs (perhaps in a trusted jurisdiction, FTA countries?). This would helps create visibility for Indian companies and a wider audience. Full fungibility on all ADRs/GDRs would also help some current companies.

I'll add more as I think of them.

Frankly, fund managers, brokers, should take a lot more interest in these market reform topics: your livelihood depends on it...It seems like people are afraid to put their head about the parapet though.

items.title: RT Rohit Shinde: Why won't wages grow? In India, wages won't grow much until we hit the Lewis Transition. The Lewis Transition point is when a develop...
items.description_html:
RT Rohit Shinde
Why won't wages grow?

In India, wages won't grow much until we hit the Lewis Transition.

The Lewis Transition point is when a developing country exhausts all the supply of rural labor that wants to move out of agriculture and into manufacturing.

Because India has 40% of its labor in agriculture, there's still a lot more labor that needs to be drained out of agriculture and into industry.

There's still a decade to go for wages to rise sustainably.


Swaraj: multiple things can be true at the same time:

- wages have not grown in the last decade or so, especially real wages and at the entry level

- you need wage suppression if you want low cost manufacturing, and our social elites will never allow it to happen since it hurts their

items.title: RT Maadhyam: Devil is in the details!! - A mischief in the #Constitution (One Hundred Thirty First) Amendment Bill, in the name of #womenreservation, ...
items.description_html:
RT Maadhyam
Devil is in the details!! - A mischief in the #Constitution (One Hundred Thirty First) Amendment Bill, in the name of #womenreservation, is to empower #Parliament to decide, from now on, which Census will be used for delimitation and how often delimitation will happen!

At present, Articles 55, 81 and 170 provide for the 'population' to be determined for the purpose of election of President, composition of #LokSabha and composition of State Assemblies respectively.

Presently, this 'population' refers to population on the basis of Census of 1971 (to determine number of seats) and 2001 census (to determine boundaries of those seats). This was frozen in the #Constitution itself, till Census after 2026 is conducted.

Originally, as provided in Articles 82 and 170, number of seats in #LokSabha and State Assemblies were supposed to be readjusted after each Census i.e. once every 10 years. But this was frozen, as is popularly known, due to difference in population growth rates across states.

Now, what is being proposed is as follows:

1. Mandate to use population numbers from Census of 1971 and 2001, till after Census of 2026 has been conducted, will be deleted.

2. It will no longer be explicitly mentioned in the Constitution that every time a Census happens, it will be followed by readjustment of seats i.e. delimitation will be delinked from census and government can choose when to conduct delimitation.

3. Parliament can bring an ordinary law to determine which Census will be used for delimitation purposes as and when it happens.

4. Women reservation will be implemented on the basis of proposed delimitation only, using whatever Census data #Parliament decides to use.

Now, the #Constitution Amendment Bill, in the text of the Bill, doesn't say that the Census of 2011 will be used for the proposed delimitation as that will now be determined by #Parliament later, by bringing another Bill. But the Statement of Objects and Reasons of the Bill says that 'latest published census' will be used, which is the 2011 Census. The Delimitation Bill (an ordinary bill) also refers to 'latest census figures'.

So, for the 2029 #LokSabha elections, the 2011 Census will be used to determine how the 850 seats, including seats reserved for women, Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes will be distributed across states. Who is likely to gain and who will lose?

But whether after Census 2026, which is also supposed to include Caste Census, another delimitation will happen, we don't know. It will no longer be mandatory to conduct delimitation after every Census. Even if delimitation happens, #Parliament can make law to say that 2011 Census will be used.

As for increase in number of seats in Legislative Assemblies, that is to be determined after delimitation is conducted and what the total number will increase to is now known yet.

items.title: “When everyone is selling, you should buy, I have understood that," 24-year old Yash Raj Verma told me, explaining why he raised by a quarter his mon...
items.description_html:
“When everyone is selling, you should buy, I have understood that," 24-year old Yash Raj Verma told me, explaining why he raised by a quarter his monthly contribution to equity investments in March.

He is among the retailers who are staying invested in the markets even as foreign investors are continuing to sell.

Who will be proven right depends on what time frame you are looking at. For many Indian SIPers this is their long-term saving plan. For foreign investors the time horizon is shorter.

This week’s India File:

items.title: RT Shashi Tharoor: The answer can be found in Europe, where the principle of degressive proportionality is applied to the composition of the European ...
items.description_html:
RT Shashi Tharoor
The answer can be found in Europe, where the principle of degressive proportionality is applied to the composition of the European Parliament — since they have the same problem of small and big states coexisting in one Union.

India also needs a compromise between strict democratic representation (one person, one vote) and the necessity of ensuring smaller political entities have a meaningful voice. It essentially means that while larger populations get more seats, the ratio of citizens to representatives increases as the population grows.

In the European Parliament, the allocation must follow these constraints:
*Minimum Threshold: No member state can have fewer than 6 seats.
*Maximum Ceiling: No member state can have more than 96 seats.
*Inverse Ratio: The "efficiency" of a vote must decrease as population increases. For example, a Member of the European Parliament (MEP) from Malta might represent roughly 80,000 citizens, while an MEP from Germany represents roughly 850,000 citizens.

The goal is to prevent the "Big Four" (Germany, France, Italy, and Spain) from holding a permanent absolute majority that could override the collective interests of the smaller nations, thereby maintaining the federalist spirit of the Union.

Applying this to India is what we need to debate, not women’s representation which no one objects to. We need to address the North-South divide that has arisen over delimitation. A strict population-based reallocation (proportional representation) would drastically increase seats for northern Hindi-belt states like Uttar Pradesh and Bihar, while states that successfully implemented population control (like Kerala and Tamil Nadu) would see their relative political influence diminish.

If India were to adopt a degressive model, the Parliament could be structured to balance population with federal equity. Similar to the EU, a "floor" could be set for smaller states (e.g., Goa, Sikkim, or the Northeast) to ensure they aren't reduced to insignificance. Instead of a fixed ratio of, say, 2 million citizens per MP, the ratio could scale. A state with 200 million people might have 2.5 million citizens per MP, while a state with 30 million might have 1 million per MP. This is to ensure no state feels disenfranchised. As @revanth_anumula suggests, another factor could be a state’s contribution to national GDP. It would be dangerous for our federalism if smaller states felt their prosperity & human development were being punished with relative disenfranchisement.

One could argue that the Rajya Sabha already exists for federal representation. However, degressive proportionality in the Lok Sabha would provide a "weighted" democratic mandate that acknowledges population without penalizing states for their developmental successes.

Finding a mathematical formula that satisfies both the high-growth and low-growth states, and both the large and small states, would require a level of bipartisan and interstate cooperation that it is in the interests of the central government to promote. I urge PM @narendramodi to initiate extensive consultations, with all parties and with all states, before rushing into a hasty delimitation process that leaves the core underlying issues unaddressed.


Varghese K George: If nothing changes in the existing constitutional scheme, southern States are set to lose their absolute AND proportional share in Lok Sabha. States with low birth rates will end up in a worse situation under the default constitutional option that will kick in after the 2027

items.title: RT Gaurab Chakrabarti: The U.S. is running Elon's playbook on LNG: Build it before the world knows it needs it. A decade ago, American LNG exports wer...
items.description_html:
RT Gaurab Chakrabarti
The U.S. is running Elon's playbook on LNG: Build it before the world knows it needs it. A decade ago, American LNG exports were near zero. Today the US is the world's largest exporter at 15 billion cubic feet per day with eight new terminals under construction. Capacity doubles to 29 billion cubic feet per day by 2029. Total capital committed: $150 billion. The contracts are 20-year take-or-pay, denominated in USD. During COVID, buyers cancelled every cargo they could. They still paid billions in fixed fees. Then Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz. Qatar, 20% of global LNG supply, went offline. Vessel transits dropped from 129 per day to 4. Europe needed a replacement. BUT the contracts were already signed. US share of European LNG: 27% in 2021, 56% today. Germany went from zero LNG imports to sourcing 94% from America. Japan is locked through 2050. American LNG is a 20-year legal obligation with no exit clause.

items.title: Does ethanol blending actually save fossil fuel consumption. India is usually compared to Brazil Sharing the math below and answer is - Not Really Key...
items.description_html:
Does ethanol blending actually save fossil fuel consumption. India is usually compared to Brazil

Sharing the math below and answer is - Not Really

Key Take Away - It makes way more sense in Brazil as their sugar cane is rain fed, while in India we use quite a bit of irrigation

India saves ~27%. Brazil saves ~87%. The difference is almost entirely irrigation — Indian sugarcane is pumped with majorly coal-fired electricity, Brazilian cane is rainfed. India's ethanol program mostly swaps imported crude for domestic coal and gas. Brazil's genuinely displaces fossil fuels.

items.title: E2E delivered a strong Q4 FY26 with revenue growth of 185.7% YoY and EBITDA margin expansion to 60.7%, demonstrating clear operating leverage from GPU...
items.description_html:
E2E delivered a strong Q4 FY26 with revenue growth of 185.7% YoY and EBITDA margin expansion to 60.7%, demonstrating clear operating leverage from GPU infrastructure investments. The company swung from quarterly loss to profit and exceeded its Gross Block guidance by 42.6%.

While full-year profitability missed guidance due to front-loaded depreciation, the Q4 inflection and strong cash flow generation indicate the business has reached a scalability threshold with significant competitive advantages in the AI cloud infrastructure market.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 185.7% YoY to ₹9,564.27 Lakhs in Q4

EBITDA surged 335.5% YoY to ₹5,810.22 Lakhs with 60.7% margin

PBT swung from loss in Q3 to profit of ₹855.82 Lakhs in Q4

Gross Block of ₹1,569 Cr exceeded guidance of ₹1,100 Cr by 42.6%

Operating cash flow at ₹12,205.68 Lakhs with 0.97x EBITDA conversion

Q4 revenue grew 36.6% QoQ showing continued momentum

Trade receivables grew 75.6% YoY indicating strong sales execution

Risk Factors

Full-year FY26 net loss of ₹1,556.59 Lakhs due to heavy depreciation

Depreciation increased 181.7% YoY to ₹16,922.69 Lakhs

EBITDA margin of 51.4% for FY26 missed guidance of 65-75%

Cash and cash equivalents decreased 30.7% YoY to ₹32,157.10 Lakhs

Other income declined 78.4% YoY impacting total income

Working capital needs may increase with rapid revenue growth

items.title: PNB Gilts reported a severe quarterly collapse with PBT down 85.7% YoY due to a massive ₹7,253 Lacs trading loss that entirely negated strong NII gro...
items.description_html:
PNB Gilts reported a severe quarterly collapse with PBT down 85.7% YoY due to a massive ₹7,253 Lacs trading loss that entirely negated strong NII growth of 113.2% YoY.

While the core interest-earning business showed robust performance with NII margin expanding 1118 bps to 25.1%, the trading book volatility confirms high interest-rate sensitivity and revenue concentration risks. The dividend increase to ₹2/share and strong capital adequacy (52.68%) provide some balance sheet comfort, but execution quality was poor with significant profit decline.

Positive Factors

NII grew 113.2% YoY to ₹10,587.18 Lacs in Q4

NII Margin expanded 1118 bps YoY to 25.1%

Total Income grew 1.4% YoY to ₹1,69,906 Lacs for FY26

Dividend increased to ₹2 per share from ₹1 in FY25

Capital Adequacy Ratio at 52.68% vs RBI requirement of 15%

Balance sheet expanded 9.5% YoY to ₹27,06,044 Lacs

Risk Factors

PAT collapsed 82.7% YoY to ₹1,299.17 Lacs in Q4

PBT fell 85.7% YoY to ₹1,416.22 Lacs in Q4

Massive trading loss of ₹7,252.77 Lacs in Q4 vs gain of ₹5,891.85 Lacs in Q4 FY25

Total expenses grew 28.3% YoY, outpacing 1.3% income growth

RoNOF declined to 11.30% from 16.46% in FY25

Fee income declined 27.8% YoY to ₹242.81 Lacs indicating diversification challenges

items.title: Navkar Corporation delivered a strong turnaround in FY26 with EBITDA of ₹118.16 crore exceeding management guidance of ₹100 crore by 18.16%. The com...
items.description_html:
Navkar Corporation delivered a strong turnaround in FY26 with EBITDA of ₹118.16 crore exceeding management guidance of ₹100 crore by 18.16%. The company transformed from a net loss of ₹45.30 crore to net profit of ₹30.15 crore, with EBITDA margin expanding by 1548 bps to 17.2%.

While working capital stress (58% receivables growth) and low RoCE (~3%) remain concerns, the execution on guidance and strong cash generation indicate improving operational efficiency.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 41.1% YoY to ₹687.46 crore

EBITDA grew 1319.9% YoY to ₹118.16 crore

EBITDA margin expanded 1548 bps YoY to 17.2%

PAT turned positive at ₹30.15 crore vs loss of ₹45.30
crore in FY25

EPS turned positive at ₹2.00 vs -₹3.01 in FY25

Beat EBITDA guidance of ₹100 crore by 18.16%

Free cash flow positive at ₹21.61 crore

Finance costs declined 23.3% YoY

Risk Factors

Trade receivables grew 58% YoY outpacing revenue growth of 41.1%

Net debt to EBITDA at 1.26x indicating moderate leverage

RoCE at ~3% remains low despite improvement

Short-term borrowings increased 49.9% YoY

Effective tax rate high at 38.1%

items.title: Bank of Maharashtra delivered an exceptional Q4 FY26 with consolidated PAT growing 36.1% YoY to ₹204.49 Crore, beating its own guidance across all ke...
items.description_html:
Bank of Maharashtra delivered an exceptional Q4 FY26 with consolidated PAT growing 36.1% YoY to ₹204.49 Crore, beating its own guidance across all key metrics including total business growth (17.5% vs 15% target) and advances growth (22.0% vs 17% target).

The bank demonstrated strong execution with margin inflections (cost-to-income at 37.1%), capital adequacy reversal (CET1 improved 149 bps QoQ to 14.59%), and asset quality improvement (GNPA at 1.45%). Retail Banking emerged as a new growth driver with PBT up 176.8% YoY.

Positive Factors

Net Profit grew 36.1% YoY to ₹204.49 Crore (consolidated) driven by 16.9% PPOP growth and 37.3% reduction in provisions

Advances growth of 22.0% YoY significantly exceeded the 17% guidance target

Total business growth of 17.5% YoY exceeded the 15% guidance target

Cost-to-income ratio improved to 37.1% for FY26, well below the sub-40% guidance

CET1 ratio rebounded to 14.59% in Q4 from 13.10% in Q3, reversing prior declining trend

Retail Banking PBT surged 176.8% YoY to ₹93.46 Crore, becoming key profit driver

Asset quality improved with GNPA at 1.45% and Net NPA at 0.13%, provision coverage at 98.59%

Capital Adequacy Ratio (CRAR) improved to 18.36% from 17.06% QoQ, well above 16% guidance

Risk Factors

Capital Adequacy (CRAR) declined 217 bps YoY to 18.36% from 20.53% despite quarterly improvement

Other Income declined 4.3% YoY to ₹93,828 lakhs in
Q4, showing volatility in non-interest income

CET1 ratio at 14.59% remains 124 bps lower than Q4 FY25 level of 15.83%

Negative cash flow from operating activities of ₹10,988.79 Crore for FY26 due to high loan book expansion

Associate loss share of ₹3.78 Crore offset subsidiary profit contribution in consolidated PAT

Impairment loss of ₹280.59 crore on Maharashtra Gramin Bank investment booked in standalone P&L

GROWW delivered exceptional Q4 FY26 with revenue up 88% YoY and PAT up 122% YoY, driven by strong operating leverage as expenses grew only 37.6% YoY. EBITDA margin expanded 1395 bps to 62.3%. Total debt reduced 69.2% YoY to ₹1,459.41 million

Employee costs rose 87.5% YoY for full year reflecting acquisition integration

items.title: Strait of Hormuz is open during ceasefire, Iran says. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the Strait of Hormuz was open following a ceasefire...
items.description_html:
Strait of Hormuz is open during ceasefire, Iran says.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the Strait of Hormuz was open following a ceasefire accord agreed in Lebanon, while US President Donald Trump said he believed a deal to end the Iran war would come "soon," although the timing remains unclear.
Araghchi said in a post on X the Strait was open for all commercial vessels for the remainder of the U.S.-brokered 10-day truce between Israeli forces and Iran-backed Hezbollah agreed between Israel and Lebanon.
He said the passage of ships would need to be along the route that Iran's Ports and Maritime Organisation had announced.
@Reuters

items.title: RT Sridhar Vembu: I am happy to see the YCombinator wave arriving in India. Our co-founder Tony and I were in silicon valley during the original YC wa...
items.description_html:
RT Sridhar Vembu
I am happy to see the YCombinator wave arriving in India. Our co-founder Tony and I were in silicon valley during the original YC wave of companies. We learned a lot from the YC companies. We also made the conscious choice to pursue a different course.

In the ancient Bharatiya tradition of philosophical debate, I am going to offer this.

I will start with things I agree with YC on.

1. The biggest lesson anyone can learn from YC: small passionate teams can do magic. This was true way before AI coding arrived and will always be true.

2. YC is absolutely right to not over-emphasize "innovation". Doing a similar product as the bigger guys, faster and cheaper, is often the best course. Google did not invent search. OpenAI did not invent the LLM. Anthropic did not invent agentic coding.

3. YC companies tend to geographically cluster, and that can lead to subtle peer pressure and group-think. So the "laggard" founders who are not growing like a weed every week start to feel left out and eventually the result is "founder depression". And YC has counselors.

By the standards of 2007 silicon valley or even 2014 silicon valley, we were thought to be losers. Just keep that in mind.

4. Many deep tech problems like building a better, cheaper MRI machine or advanced semiconductor equipment require long focus and patient execution and lots of capital. These are endurance tests, not weekly sprints.

5. YC too often optimizes companies for "exit". That philosophy was built for and requires prolonged bubbles, which American policy has delivered, at the price of nearly wrecking the country (note the extreme inequality and political division). If you love India, you should not wish for similar bubbles.

6. YC model worked in silicon valley. One of the reasons it worked was that silicon valley could get any talent from anywhere in the world, notably from India, easily. That era may have ended or at least on pause right now.

Bengaluru has tried the same thing but with "any talent from anywhere in India" and we have not yet created huge companies. India needs its Huawei and Xiaomi and BYD and these companies are Chinese to the core, built by patriotic Chinese. Indian talent, staying in India, rooted in India, is going to have to build companies like them. Enough said.


Jared Friedman: What I told 2,000 future founders in Bengaluru today:

1/ We believe we are at the start of a second wave of Indian companies that will build world-class AI native products for the global market. Emergent and Giga are the model of the future.

2/ Just because a space seems

items.title: Paytm owner One97 Communications has now become an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC). Until now, IOCC status has been significant for online ...
items.description_html:
Paytm owner One97 Communications has now become an Indian-owned and controlled company (IOCC).

Until now, IOCC status has been significant for online retailers, as it allows them to hold inventory.

Eternal-owned Blinkit became one through a QIP, Swiggy is close to getting there, and Nykaa has always had this advantage.

Paytm is among the first major listed fintechs to get IOCC status.

There may not be immediate, direct business benefits, but this is important given the scrutiny Paytm has faced over ownership by China’s Alibaba and Ant Financial, which together held 38% before the IPO.

After the IPO in 2021, foreign investors held 81%, which has now come down to just below 50%.

items.title: 🚨 Superhealth and Even Healthcare are among a group of startups testing hospital models built on smaller facilities, lower upfront investment, and ...
items.description_html:
🚨 Superhealth and Even Healthcare are among a group of startups testing hospital models built on smaller facilities, lower upfront investment, and standardised pricing.

Their thesis isn’t that conventional hospitals or large chains can be replaced, but that short-stay and elective care can be delivered more efficiently through a leaner model.

Instead of replicating the full-service, capital-intensive format of multi-speciality hospitals, they are building 50–70 bed facilities focused on procedures with an average length of stay (ALOS) of under two nights.

This narrower clinical scope, they believe, enables faster patient turnover and keeps per-bed capital costs in check.

In the past, these efforts haven't worked, but these startups are looking to set a new precedent.

https://www.thearcweb.com/article/superhealth-even-healthcare-hospitals-medical-services-funding-jZaCa17UwFMDsJl8

items.title: Venture firm Nexus has sold shares worth Rs 530 crore in logistics company Delhivery, generating about 15x returns. Nexus first invested in Delhivery ...
items.description_html:
Venture firm Nexus has sold shares worth Rs 530 crore in logistics company Delhivery, generating about 15x returns.

Nexus first invested in Delhivery back in 2013 and did not sell any shares during the IPO in 2022.

While Delhivery’s share price has been sluggish since listing, it is now inching closer to its Rs 487 IPO price. The shares were sold for Rs 442.

Nexus will continue to hold about a 2.8% stake in Delhivery after this round of sales - worth about Rs 1,000 crore at the current market cap of Rs 35,000 crore or $3.8 billion.

Another pre-IPO investor, SoftBank, continues to hold about an 8% stake.

items.title: RT Nithin Kamath: I recently had dinner with Dr Devi Shetty, the founder of Narayana Hospitals. For those who don't know him, he's the guy who figured...
items.description_html:
RT Nithin Kamath
I recently had dinner with Dr Devi Shetty, the founder of Narayana Hospitals. For those who don't know him, he's the guy who figured out how to do open heart surgery for a few hundred dollars when the same procedure costs a bomb in the US. Narayana has 18,000 beds across India, and if you ask most middle-class people in Bangalore about it, they'll speak highly of it.

There was one thing I kept thinking about over and over again after meeting him.

Narayana's market cap is around ₹38,000 crore. Now compare that to pretty much any half-decent financial services business in India, and it'll be valued more than that, including Zerodha. A brokerage, worth more than a hospital chain, that has probably saved hundreds of thousands of lives.

I get the arguments. If you're a fund manager/analyst, you can immediately explain it away using margins, capex, asset-light vs asset-heavy, and all that, and I'm not saying the market is wrong.

But it's still a strange world we've built, where the businesses closest to money get valued the highest, and the ones doing the hard and essential things get priced like boring utilities. A hospital carries physical infrastructure, enormous liability, thin margins and the actual weight of keeping people alive. And somehow that's worth less than a platform for buying and selling stocks.

I don't have a clean take on this. All of this just felt odd.

Ps: Nothing here is investment advice. For that, go to @zerodhavarsity

items.title: Zepto has received in-principle approval from the market regulator SEBI for its IPO and is targeting a listing as early as June this year. This is imp...
items.description_html:
Zepto has received in-principle approval from the market regulator SEBI for its IPO and is targeting a listing as early as June this year.

This is important as it comes at a time when market volatility has pushed PhonePe to delay its IPO, despite reducing its valuation expectation by over 30% from $15 billion to $10 billion.

However, PhonePe can hold back its IPO because its entire issue was an offer for sale, and it can always rely on its parent company, Walmart, for additional funding.

But Zepto needs primary capital.

That’s because it has about $600–700 million in cash in the bank as of March 2026, while both key rivals, Blinkit (Eternal) and Swiggy, are sitting on close to a $2 billion war chest.

Like Swiggy, Zepto’s burn rate remains elevated as it focuses on higher-order volumes as compared to AOV growth chased by other rivals.

So, Zepto needs fresh capital — likely through an IPO or a private funding round — but valuation will come under pressure in both cases.

Its last private valuation was $7 billion. The price discovery process is underway — the next few weeks will be interesting.

https://www.thearcweb.com/article/zepto-sebi-ipo-aadit-palicha-quick-commerce-CLqE7WXLua4zj0iR
